intro.kicker: Topic 1 of 4 • Chapter: Work, Energy, Power and Collision • Unit: Work, Energy and Power • Physics
intro.description: Work is said to be done when a force applied on the body displaces the body through a certain distance in the direction of force. This topic covers Introduction, Work Done by a Constant Force, Dimension and Units of Work and Nature of Work Done. Key principles include: Scalar (dot) product formula: W = F·s = Fs cosθ; Multiple forces: W = (F₁ + F₂ + ... + Fₙ)·(r₂ - r₁). In NEET Physics, Work Done is tested directly and as part of multi-concept problems. Mastery requires both conceptual clarity and strong numerical practice.

expectedQuestions.description: Work Done features in NEET Physics as a direct question or embedded in multi-step problems. Detailed study of Work, Energy, Power and Collision is recommended.

timeRequired.description: Theory 10–15 min, plus solving 45 practice problems. Efficient if you know the formula derivation.

difficulty.description: Work, Energy, Power and Collision difficulty level. Mastery requires understanding conditions of validity, dimensional analysis, and numerical practice with NEET-style traps.

nriUsaGap.description: Work Done has overlap with AP Physics curriculum, though NEET requires deeper formula derivation and multi-step numerical mastery beyond standard AP Physics expectations.

bulletNotes: Work Done appears regularly in NEET Physics; never skip it.
Often combined with adjacent topics in multi-step problems.
Master formula derivation and conceptual application for best results.

strategySteps.title: Concept First
strategySteps.description: Understand the physical meaning behind Work Done. Derive key formulae from first principles so you can reconstruct them under exam pressure.

strategySteps.title: Formula Mapping
strategySteps.description: List all formulae for this topic on a single sheet. Note the conditions under which each applies. NEET frequently tests boundary conditions and exceptions.

strategySteps.title: Practice NEET-style MCQs
strategySteps.description: Solve at least 20 past-paper-style questions on this topic. Note the traps: unit mismatches, sign errors, and direction conventions account for most wrong answers.

cards.title: Core Concept: Work Done
cards.coreRevision: Work Done is a key pillar of Work, Energy, Power and Collision in NEET Physics. Understanding its definitions, derivations, and standard results is essential for solving both theory and numerical questions.
points: Work Done is a fundamental concept in Work, Energy, Power and Collision
Always verify units and dimensional consistency

cards.title: NEET Traps to Avoid
cards.coreRevision: NEET setters target conceptual gaps in Work Done. The most common errors involve sign conventions, unit mismatches, and misapplication of conditions under which formulae are valid.
points: Check sign conventions carefully (vector vs scalar quantities)
Verify that all conditions for the formula are satisfied
Dimensional analysis can catch formula errors instantly
cards.example: In NEET, Work Done questions often combine with adjacent chapter concepts. Practice multi-step problems to expose hidden links.

curriculum.hintText: Topics in Work Done covered differently or not at all in AP Physics vs NEET.

gaps.title: Work Done in US vs Indian Curriculum
gaps.description: Work-energy-power and collisions are well-covered globally. NEET adds collision-type classification problems that require knowing conditions for elastic vs inelastic in specific scenarios.
bullets: NEET expects derivation-level mastery; AP Physics often treats Work Done as applied only.
NRI students should supplement textbook study with NEET-specific problem sets.
Pay special attention to SI unit standards and sign conventions which differ from US conventions.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes Work Done in the context of NEET Physics?

options.text: Work Done applies only under idealized conditions with no friction or air resistance
Work Done is a fundamental result derivable from first principles and applies within well-defined physical conditions
Work Done has no mathematical formulation and is purely qualitative
Work Done contradicts Newton's laws in non-inertial frames

questions.explanation: Work Done is a rigorously derived physical principle within Work, Energy, Power and Collision. It has a precise mathematical form, applies under specified conditions, and is fully consistent with Newton's laws. NEET frequently tests whether students can distinguish domain of applicability from physical principle validity.

questions.question: In the study of Work, Energy, Power and Collision, which statement about Work Done by a Constant Force is CORRECT for a NEET Physics student?

options.text: It is only tested as an isolated topic and never combined with energy or momentum problems
The dimensional formula does not follow from SI base units
It is integral to Work, Energy, Power and Collision and appears in direct and multi-concept NEET questions
It is outside the NEET Physics syllabus since 2020

questions.explanation: Work Done by a Constant Force is a core component of Work, Energy, Power and Collision and regularly appears in NEET. Multi-step problems combining this with energy, momentum, or kinematics are common. Dimensional analysis confirms SI unit consistency. This topic has remained in the NEET syllabus continuously.

questions.question: In a NEET context, when is Work Done most directly applicable?
options: Only in ideal, frictionless, zero-gravity scenarios
Whenever the stated conditions for Work Done are satisfied, including in standard NEET problems
questions.answer: Whenever the stated conditions for Work Done are satisfied, including in standard NEET problems

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the dimensional formula of the primary physical quantity in Work Done?
options: It follows from SI base units with standard MLT notation
It requires non-SI units for proper expression
Dimensional analysis is inapplicable to this quantity
questions.answer: It follows from SI base units with standard MLT notation

questions.question: The most common NEET trap in Work Done problems is:
options: Applying the formula outside its domain of validity or with incorrect sign conventions
questions.answer: Applying the formula outside its domain of validity or with incorrect sign conventions

items.title: Define Work Done precisely
items.description: Write out the complete definition of Work Done including all qualifying conditions and the physical quantities involved.

items.title: Derive the key formula from first principles
items.description: Reproduce the main derivation without notes. Understanding each step ensures you can identify which formula applies under non-standard NEET conditions.

items.title: State conditions of validity
items.description: List all assumptions and conditions under which Work Done results hold. NEET setters test these boundaries.

items.title: Master Introduction
items.description: Solve at least 5 NEET-style problems specifically on Introduction to ensure pattern recognition and speed.

items.title: Master Work Done by a Constant Force
items.description: Solve at least 5 NEET-style problems specifically on Work Done by a Constant Force to ensure pattern recognition and speed.

items.question: How many questions from Work Done appear in NEET each year?
items.answer: Work Done typically contributes 0–2 questions per NEET paper depending on the year. While it may not appear every year, it often features in multi-concept problems linked to adjacent topics in Work, Energy, Power and Collision. Skipping it is risky.

items.question: Is Work Done in the NEET 2025 syllabus?
items.answer: Yes, Work Done remains part of the NEET Physics syllabus under Work, Energy, Power and Collision. The National Medical Commission periodically updates the syllabus, so always verify with the latest NMC/NTA official syllabus notification before your exam.

items.question: What is the best way to revise Work Done before NEET?
items.answer: Use a three-pass strategy: (1) Read the derivation and concept; (2) Solve 10 NEET-style MCQs; (3) On the day before exam, review only the formulae sheet and your flagged error patterns from practice. Focus on conditions of validity and sign conventions.

items.question: What is the most common mistake in Work Done problems?
items.answer: The most frequent errors in Work Done are: applying the formula outside its domain (e.g., using ideal-gas results for real gases), sign errors in vector quantities, and ignoring units in multi-step calculations. Practice specifically designed trap questions to inoculate against these errors.

grounding_summary: Work Done is covered in the assigned page range of pages/physics-1/ as part of Work, Energy, Power and Collision.
The local OCR pages establish definitions, key concepts, and formulae for Work Done in the NEET Physics context.
Chapter research from Exa confirms current NEET exam patterns and authoritative sources for Work Done.
grounding_excerpt: Work is said to be done when a force applied on the body displaces the body through a certain distance in the direction of force.
Thus work done by a force is equal to the scalar (or dot) product of the force and the displacement of the body.
Positive work means that force (or its component) is parallel to displacement
